patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: write-protect folios during data writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: write-protect folios during data writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds write-protection for folios during data writeback in the Btrfs file system to prevent corruption and data loss due to concurrent writes by processes with an mmap-ed file. The patch addresses issues related to large folios, subpage sectorsize, zoned submissions, inline extents, and EOF-spanning folios, where a process can modify a sector while it is being used by writeback without faulting, leading to corruption or lost writes.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a task with an existing mmap-ed PTE writes to a folio before it is fully submitted and the dirty bit is cleared, which can result in losing the write even if fsync() is called.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init
[PATCH v2 0/3] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] fuse: fix missing barrier when checking io-uring readiness
series_items.patch_summary: The patch fixes a potential deadlock in the fuse kernel module by adding a read barrier to ensure that io-uring queues are ready before allocating requests. This prevents a CPU from observing inconsistent state between fch->initialized and fch->io_uring, which could lead to a lock-order inversion deadlock.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Suggested removing the smp_rmb() barrier in fuse_get_req(), as it follows fuse_block_alloc().
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/15/26 19:43, Joanne Koong wrote:
> fuse_block_alloc() reads fch->initialized and then fch->io_uring.
> fch->io_uring is set before fch->initialized, ordered by the smp_wmb()
> in fuse_chan_set_intialized(), but fuse_block_alloc() has no matching
> read barrier between the two loads.
> 
> This may lead a CPU to observe fch->initialized=1 but fch->io_uring=0,
> and skip the check that blocks request allocation until the io-uring
> queues are ready. This can reintroduce the lock-order inversion deadlock
> that commit 3393ff964e0f prevents.
> 
> Add an smp_rmb() barrier to pair with the smp_wmb() in
> fuse_chan_set_initialized() to prevent this.
> 
> Fixes: 3393ff964e0f ("fuse: block request allocation until io-uring init is complete")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> ---
>  fs/fuse/dev.c | 8 +++++++-
>  1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/dev.c b/fs/fuse/dev.c
> index 5763a7cd3b37..b70c536d7e25 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/dev.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/dev.c
> @@ -85,7 +85,13 @@ void fuse_chan_set_initialized(struct fuse_chan *fch, struct fuse_chan_param *pa
>  
>  static bool fuse_block_alloc(struct fuse_chan *fch, bool for_background)
>  {
> -	return !fch->initialized || (for_background && fch->blocked) ||
> +	if (!fch->initialized)
> +		return true;
> +
> +	/* Pairs with smp_wmb() in fuse_chan_set_initialized() */
> +	smp_rmb();
> +
> +	return (for_background && fch->blocked) ||
>  	       (fch->io_uring && fch->connected && !fuse_uring_ready(fch));
>  }
>  

I wonder if we could remove smp_rmb() in fuse_get_req(), it follows
fuse_block_alloc().

Otherwise,

Reviewed-by: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] fuse: use release/acquire for fch->initialized
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module where lockless readers may access the 'initialized' flag of a fuse channel without proper synchronization, leading to undefined behavior. To fix this, the patch introduces release/acquire semantics for writing and reading the 'initialized' flag using smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire(). This ensures that writers and readers are properly synchronized, preventing potential issues. The changes are made in several files: cuse.c, dev.c, and dev_uring.c. Although this issue is not considered critical and may only be triggered by buggy or malicious servers, the patch aims to improve the overall robustness of the FUSE module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] fuse: publish io-uring queues with release semantics
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new feature to publish io-uring queues with release semantics, ensuring that the queue is fully initialized before its pointer is accessed by readers. This fix addresses a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page() where the folio refcount drops to zero prematurely.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Recommended adding READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE in fuse_uring_destruct for consistency. Provided a Reviewed-by tag.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses missing barriers in the io-uring initialization of a fuse device, fixing issues reported by Sashiko. The patches modify fuse code to use release/acquire semantics for fch->initialized and add barriers where necessary.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Recommended using READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE in fuse_uring_destruct() for consistency and added another Reviewed-by tag.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 1/3] fuse: whitelist the request headers for usercopy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch whitelists request headers for usercopy in the fuse module, allowing them to be safely accessed by userspace without requiring additional memory copies.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the extra hop through a tmp stack variable is negligible due to small memcpys (~40 bytes) and memory being in L1 cache, but didn't express a strong opinion
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for late replies, currently on vacation and reviews only when
everyone else is asleep.

I don't have a strong opinion, I wondered the same if the stack copy was
avoidable, but then came to the same conclusion as Joanne - negligible
overhead.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] fuse: select alternative io_uring queue if local queue is full
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch aims to select an alternative io_uring queue if the local queue is full, but a similar approach already exists in Bernd's reduced queues patchset.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Identified that the patch's goal is already covered by Bernd's reduced queues patchset, suggesting it may not be necessary to submit a new patch.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged that the patch has a similar goal to their own reduced queues patchset, but noted it's a slightly different approach and promised to submit an updated version once back from vacation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/15/26 19:51, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Tue, Jul 14, 2026 at 12:33\u202fAM Li Wang <liwang@kylinos.cn> wrote:
>>
>> fuse_uring_task_to_queue() always maps requests to the queue for
>> task_cpu(current). A single-threaded submitter with high queue depth
>> could leave other per-CPU queues idle while one queue handles all
>> traffic.
>>
>> Prefer the local queue when it has available ring entries. If the local
>> queue is full, try other queues on the same NUMA node before falling
>> back to use the local queue.
> 
> Hi Li,
> 
> I think this is already covered by Bernd's changes in his reduced
> queues patchset [1].
> 
> Thanks,
> Joanne
> 
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/

It is a slightly different approach, but both have the same goal
to get more balanced queue/core usage.

I will submit a new version once I'm back from vacation.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v1 0/2] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init
Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch disables the default strictlimiting of block device I/O (bdi) for FUSE file systems, which can lead to inaccurate dirty page statistics and excessive writeback. The issue arises from periodic flushing of memory cgroup stats, causing lagging values that result in under-reporting of dirty pages. This can cause writers to exceed the limit by more than 2x before throttling kicks in. The patch aims to address this problem by disabling strictlimiting for FUSE file systems.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the balance_dirty_pages() code uses lagging memcg stats, leading to under-reported values and potential overrunning of the dirty limit, suggesting a possible solution to flush based on the number of pages getting dirtied instead of elapsed time.

Reviewer noted that the recent change to use mem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited() in mem_cgroup_wb_stats() causes applications to overshoot memcg dirty limits by several gigabytes, particularly for small memcgs, and suggested reverting this change or taking into account the size of memcg when ratelimiting.
review_comments.raw_body:
I see. That would indeed explain why we can overrun the dirty limit so
much.

---

Checking some git history, this actually seems to be a relatively recent
change from Shakeel (added to CC) - d9b3ce8769e3 ("mm: writeback: ratelimit
stat flush from mem_cgroup_wb_stats"). So you can check whether reverting
that change makes dirty throttling not overshoot so much.

Shakeel, your change to use mem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited() in
mem_cgroup_wb_stats() results in applications overshooting memcg dirty
limits by several gigabytes which for small memcgs (with 8GB memory in this
case, which means a dirty limit of ~1.5GB) is a considerable issue. I guess
the ratelimiting should take into account the size of memcg (or ideally the
dirty limit in the case of mem_cgroup_wb_stats()) as well. Using
somewhat outdated numbers for dirty throttling is fine but when we can
overshoot the dirty limit more than three times, it leads to rather long
stalls in dirty throttling and similar issues...

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Jan Kara pointed out that the patch description does not provide a clear explanation for why the default bdi strictlimiting is being disabled and requested more context.

Reviewer Jan Kara acknowledged that the issue of overrunning the dirty limit is explained by the periodic flushing of memcg stats, and noted that this could be addressed by modifying the throttle path to use page count instead of time elapsed.

Reviewer noted that Shakeel's recent change to use mem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited() in mem_cgroup_wb_stats() results in applications overshooting memcg dirty limits by several gigabytes, particularly for small memcgs. He suggested reverting the change and also proposed modifying the ratelimiting to take into account the size of the memcg or its dirty limit.
review_comments.raw_body:
[resending once more, this time with correct Shakeel's address]

On Tue 14-07-26 18:21:57, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

I see. That would indeed explain why we can overrun the dirty limit so
much.

---

Checking some git history, this actually seems to be a relatively recent
change from Shakeel (added to CC) - d9b3ce8769e3 ("mm: writeback: ratelimit
stat flush from mem_cgroup_wb_stats"). So you can check whether reverting
that change makes dirty throttling not overshoot so much.

Shakeel, your change to use mem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited() in
mem_cgroup_wb_stats() results in applications overshooting memcg dirty
limits by several gigabytes which for small memcgs (with 8GB memory in this
case, which means a dirty limit of ~1.5GB) is a considerable issue. I guess
the ratelimiting should take into account the size of memcg (or ideally the
dirty limit in the case of mem_cgroup_wb_stats()) as well. Using
somewhat outdated numbers for dirty throttling is fine but when we can
overshoot the dirty limit more than three times, it leads to rather long
stalls in dirty throttling and similar issues...

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Jan Kara's concern about high dirty page counts exceeding limits by reverting a specific commit (d9b3ce8769e3) which fixes the issue, resulting in bounded dirty pages and reduced latency.

Author acknowledges that reverting commit d9b3ce8769e3 might be the simplest fix due to reduced global lock contention, but is open to exploring alternative solutions if reverting does not address the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Reverting d9b3ce8769e3 fixes the issue. On xfs, I'm now seeing the
number of dirty pages stays bounded by the limit instead of going 2x+
over, and the max latency is now around ~0.33s instead of ~4s.

---

It looks like commit d9b3ce8769e3 was landed in Feb 2024 when rstat
was still using the single global lock. I see commit 748922dcfabd
("cgroup: use subsystem-specific rstat locks to avoid contention")
later in the tree, which was landed around May 2025 and reduces that
global lock contention. That might make the cost of the flush a lot
lower than what it was when the ratelimit was needed in d9b3ce8769e3,
so maybe reverting d9b3ce8769e3 wouldn't make much of a difference for
write-heavy workloads and would be the simplest fix? If it does still
regress, I'd be happy to look into making the flush based on how many
pages have been dirtied and scaled to the dirty limit, if that sounds
like the best way forward. I tried running the sysbench
oltp_write_only benchmark mentioned in d9b3ce8769e3 on my setup but
couldn't get the database to dirty pages fast enough to trigger the
balance dirty pages logic to see how it performs without the
ratelimiting. If you still have the script for how you ran the test
back in 2024, I'm happy to give it a go on my local machine.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 3/3] fuse: deduplicate the oversized-request error selection
